heb.paragraph: The phrase chilia etē (a thousand years) appears six times in this passage (vv. 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7), making it the most concentrated use of a specific time period in Revelation. The interpretation of this phrase is the primary dividing line between the major eschatological systems: premillennialism reads it as a literal future period; amillennialism reads it as symbolic for the entire church age; postmillennialism reads it as an extended period of gospel triumph before Christ's return. The Old Testament background includes Psalm 90:4 ('a thousand years in your sight are like a day') and the rabbinic tradition of a messianic sabbath following six millennia of history.

The phrase hē anastasis hē prōtē (the first resurrection) is unique to this passage and generates intense interpretive debate. Premillennialists read it as a physical, bodily resurrection of believers preceding the millennium. Amillennialists typically read it as spiritual resurrection (regeneration or the soul's entry into heavenly reign at death), following Augustine's influential interpretation. The adjective prōtē (first) implies a corresponding second, and the second resurrection is associated with the 'second death' (v. 6), suggesting a contrast between life-giving and judgment-leading resurrections.

panels.ctx: Revelation 20:1–6 is arguably the most debated passage in the entire book, generating the three major eschatological systems that have divided Christianity for centuries. The binding of Satan (vv. 1–3) by an angel with 'the key to the Abyss and a great chain' raises the question of whether this is a future event following the second coming (premillennial view), a present reality initiated by Christ's first coming (amillennial view), or a future period of increasing gospel success (postmillennial view). The 'thousand years' during which the martyrs reign with Christ (vv. 4–6) is the central interpretive crux. The 'first resurrection' (v. 5) is either physical (bodily resurrection of believers before the millennium) or spiritual (regeneration or heavenly enthronement), with significant grammatical and contextual arguments on both sides. The fifth beatitude—'Blessed and holy are those who share in the first resurrection' (v. 6)—assures participants that 'the second death has no power over them,' linking resurrection status to immunity from final judgment. The passage must be read in light of its broader canonical context: Daniel 7:9–27, Isaiah 24–27, Ezekiel 37–39, and 1 Corinthians 15:20–28.

cross.note: Isaiah prophesied that 'the LORD will punish the powers in the heavens above and the kings on the earth below. They will be herded together like prisoners bound in a dungeon; they will be shut up in prison and be punished after many days.' This passage provides Old Testament precedent for a temporary imprisonment of evil powers followed by subsequent judgment—a sequence that parallels the binding of Satan for a thousand years followed by his release and final defeat.

Daniel's vision of thrones set in place and the court sitting in judgment provides the background for the thrones of Revelation 20:4. Daniel 7:22 specifies that 'judgment was given in favor of the holy people of the Most High, and the time came when they possessed the kingdom.' The millennial reign of the saints (Rev 20:4–6) fulfills this Danielic promise of the saints' participation in divine judgment and governance.

Paul's resurrection sequence—'Christ, the firstfruits; then, when he comes, those who belong to him. Then the end comes'—has been read both as supporting a two-stage resurrection (premillennial) and as describing a single event at Christ's return (amillennial). Either reading connects to the 'first resurrection' of Revelation 20:5–6, indicating that the resurrection hope is central to the millennium debate.

notes.note: The binding of Satan is a literal, future event that follows the second coming described in 19:11–21. The angel's great chain and the sealed Abyss represent the complete removal of satanic influence from the earth during the millennial kingdom. Satan's binding is not merely a limitation of his activity (as the amillennial view holds) but a total cessation: he is unable to 'deceive the nations anymore' (v. 3). The present age, in which Satan is described as 'the god of this age' (2 Cor 4:4) and a 'roaring lion' (1 Pet 5:8), cannot be the millennium.

The thrones and those seated on them represent glorified saints who will reign with Christ over the earth during the thousand-year kingdom. The 'souls of those who had been beheaded' specifically identifies the tribulation martyrs, who are resurrected bodily to participate in the millennial reign. This is the fulfillment of Christ's promise that the meek will inherit the earth (Matt 5:5).

The 'first resurrection' is physical and bodily—the same word (anastasis) used for Christ's resurrection and for the general resurrection. If the second resurrection is physical (v. 12–13), consistency demands that the first resurrection is also physical. The second death has no power over those who share in the first resurrection—they are eternally secure, beyond the reach of final judgment.

Calvin interpreted the thousand years symbolically as representing the entire period between Christ's first and second advents. The binding of Satan does not mean the cessation of all satanic activity but the restriction of his ability to prevent the gospel from reaching the nations. Before Christ, Satan held the Gentile world in darkness; after the cross and resurrection, the gospel goes forth to all nations (Matt 28:18–20). The binding is real but limited—Satan is restrained from the specific activity of preventing gospel advance, though he continues to persecute and tempt within God's sovereign permission.

Calvin read the 'first resurrection' as the spiritual resurrection of the soul—regeneration and participation in Christ's risen life (cf. John 5:24–25; Eph 2:5–6; Col 3:1). The saints 'reign with Christ' not in a future earthly kingdom but in the present heavenly session: those who die in Christ immediately enter the blessed state of reigning with Him. The 'second death' is the final, eternal separation from God that affects only those who have not participated in the first (spiritual) resurrection.

The four titles—ton drakonta, ton ophin ton archaion, hos estin Diabolos kai ho Satanas (the dragon, the ancient serpent, who is the devil and Satan)—recapitulate 12:9, creating a full identification. Each title evokes a different aspect: dragon (mythic chaos monster), ancient serpent (Genesis 3 tempter), devil (slanderer), Satan (adversary). The accumulated titles leave no ambiguity about the identity of the imprisoned figure.

The phrase hoi loipoi tōn nekrōn ouk ezēsan (the rest of the dead did not come to life) is parenthetical—some manuscripts omit it, suggesting possible scribal addition, though the majority of witnesses include it. If original, it distinguishes two categories of dead: those who participate in the first resurrection (v. 4) and 'the rest' who do not live again until the thousand years are ended.

Beale advocates the amillennial reading: the thousand years symbolize the entire inter-advent period, and the binding of Satan represents Christ's victory at the cross and resurrection (cf. Matt 12:28–29; John 12:31; Col 2:15). The 'first resurrection' is the spiritual resurrection of believers who, upon death, enter the heavenly reign with Christ—they 'came to life' (ezēsan) in the sense of entering heavenly existence, paralleling the souls under the altar in 6:9–11. Beale argues that the thousand years should not be pressed as a chronological period following chapter 19 but as a recapitulation of the church age from a new perspective—just as chapters 12–14 recapitulated the inter-advent period from the perspective of the satanic trinity's assault on the church. The key exegetical argument is that 'the rest of the dead did not come to life' (v. 5) uses ezēsan in the same sense as v. 4—spiritual participation in Christ's reign—and that the 'second death' (v. 6) is the counterpart of the first resurrection, confirming the spiritual interpretation.

Osborne represents a modified premillennial position, reading the thousand years as a future period of Christ's earthly reign following the second coming of chapter 19. He argues that the sequential narrative markers ('and I saw,' kai eidon) in 19:11–20:15 establish chronological progression—the millennium follows the parousia, which follows Babylon's fall. The 'first resurrection' is bodily, parallel to Christ's own bodily resurrection, and the thousand years are a literal (though possibly rounded) period during which Satan's deception of the nations is suspended. Osborne acknowledges the amillennial reading as a serious exegetical option but contends that the most natural reading of the text—physical resurrection, chronological sequence, literal imprisonment—favors the premillennial interpretation. He notes that the passage deliberately leaves many details unspecified, focusing on the theological realities of Satan's restraint, the saints' vindication, and the final resolution of evil.

heb.paragraph: The names Gōg kai Magōg derive from Ezekiel 38–39, where Gog is a ruler from the land of Magog who leads a vast coalition against Israel in the latter days, only to be utterly destroyed by divine intervention. In Ezekiel, these are specific geopolitical entities; in Revelation, they have become symbolic designations for the totality of the nations deceived by Satan for the final assault against God's people. The transformation from specific to universal reflects John's consistent hermeneutical practice of typological universalization: what Ezekiel described as a regional conflict, John expands to encompass the entire world's final rebellion.

panels.ctx: The release of Satan after the thousand years (v. 7) initiates the final eschatological drama. Satan immediately resumes his primary activity—deception of the nations—and gathers Gog and Magog for the ultimate assault against 'the camp of God's people, the city he loves' (v. 9). The names Gog and Magog derive from Ezekiel 38–39 but are universalized: they no longer represent specific northern nations but the totality of rebellious humanity 'in the four corners of the earth' (v. 8). The comparison to 'the sand on the seashore' (v. 8) echoes the Abrahamic promise (Gen 22:17), creating a bitter irony—the number intended for blessing now describes the forces arrayed against God's people. The resolution is swift and decisive: fire comes down from heaven and devours them (v. 9), echoing Elijah's fire from heaven (2 Kgs 1:10–12) and Ezekiel's judgment on Gog (Ezek 38:22; 39:6). The devil is thrown into the lake of fire where the beast and false prophet already reside (v. 10), completing the destruction of the satanic trinity. The torment is described as 'day and night for ever and ever' (eis tous aiōnas tōn aiōnōn), the strongest possible expression of eternal duration in Greek.

cross.note: Ezekiel's Gog oracle provides the primary background for Revelation 20:7–10. In Ezekiel, Gog leads a vast coalition from the north against restored Israel and is destroyed by divine intervention (fire, brimstone, plague, torrential rain). Revelation universalizes this oracle: Gog and Magog represent all the nations of the earth gathered for the final rebellion, and fire from heaven devours them all.

Elijah called fire down from heaven to consume the soldiers sent to arrest him. The fire from heaven that devours Gog and Magog (Rev 20:9) echoes this prophetic tradition of divine fire as the instrument of judgment against those who oppose God and His servants.

notes.note: Satan's release after the millennium demonstrates that a perfect environment does not produce perfect hearts. Even after a thousand years of Christ's righteous rule, when Satan is released, he finds willing followers among the nations. This is the ultimate proof that human rebellion is not the product of environment or education but of the fallen human heart. The number gathered—'like the sand on the seashore'—indicates a massive, global coalition.

The attack on 'the camp of God's people, the city he loves' targets Jerusalem, the capital of the millennial kingdom. But there is no battle. Fire comes down from heaven and devours the attackers. God does not negotiate with rebellion; He destroys it. This parallels the destruction of Sodom (Gen 19:24) and the Gog of Ezekiel (Ezek 38:22; 39:6).

The devil is thrown into the lake of fire where the beast and false prophet have been for a thousand years—still alive, still tormented, still conscious. The phrase 'tormented day and night for ever and ever' is the most explicit statement of eternal conscious punishment in Scripture. The satanic trinity meets its eternal fate in the lake prepared for them (Matt 25:41).

Satan's release and final rebellion reveal that his binding during the millennium was a restraint, not a transformation. Evil is not reformed but restrained; when the restraint is removed, it immediately reasserts itself. Calvin would find here confirmation that apart from sovereign grace, the human heart inclines naturally toward rebellion regardless of circumstances. The brevity of the final conflict—fire from heaven, no combat—demonstrates God's absolute supremacy. The eternal torment of the devil, beast, and false prophet in the lake of fire is not excessive but proportionate: rebellion against an infinite God warrants infinite consequences.

The phrase eis tous tessaras gōnias tēs gēs (to the four corners of the earth) is a cosmological expression indicating universality, not literal geography. The comparison hōn ho arithmos autōn hōs hē ammos tēs thalassēs (whose number is like the sand of the sea) ironically echoes the Abrahamic blessing formula (Gen 22:17; 32:12), now applied to the enemies of God's people rather than to Abraham's descendants.

The phrase basanisthēsontai hēmeras kai nyktos eis tous aiōnas tōn aiōnōn (they will be tormented day and night forever and ever) uses the strongest available Greek formula for eternal duration. The future passive basanisthēsontai (they will be tormented) is a divine passive, indicating God as the ultimate agent of the torment.

Beale reads Satan's release and the Gog-Magog conflict as the final, brief intensification of persecution that precedes Christ's return—corresponding to the 'little while' of 6:11 and 12:12. On the amillennial reading, the thousand years represent the entire church age; Satan's release represents the final, intensified period of deception immediately before the end. The fire from heaven (v. 9) fulfills Ezekiel 38:22 and 39:6, confirming that John is presenting the Gog-Magog oracle as a type of the final eschatological conflict. The lake of fire (v. 10) represents the irreversible, eternal exclusion of evil from God's creation—the definitive solution to the problem of evil that the new creation (21–22) will embody.

Osborne notes that the Gog-Magog rebellion answers a theological question implicit in the millennium: if Satan is bound and Christ reigns, why does evil persist? The answer is that Satan's binding prevents deception of the nations but does not eliminate the sinful nature. Upon his release, the latent rebellion surfaces immediately and massively. The fire from heaven resolves the conflict without combat, consistent with the pattern of 19:19–21 where Christ's word alone defeats the enemy. The eternal torment of the satanic trinity (v. 10) is the ultimate theodicy: evil is not merely defeated but permanently, consciously excluded from God's renewed creation.

heb.paragraph: The adjective megas (great) indicates supreme authority; leukos (white) indicates purity and righteousness. Together, they describe a throne of absolute, unimpeachable justice. The singular thronos contrasts with the plural thronoi of 20:4—there, delegated authority; here, ultimate authority. The identity of the one seated is not specified, but the context implies God the Father or Christ as the divine judge (cf. John 5:22–27, where the Father entrusts all judgment to the Son). Earth and heaven 'flee from his presence' (v. 11)—the old creation cannot endure the unveiled holiness of the judge.

heb.word: λίμνην τοῦ πυρός... ὁ θάνατος ὁ δεύτερος
heb.transliteration: limnēn tou pyros... ho thanatos ho deuteros
heb.gloss: lake of fire... the second death
heb.paragraph: The noun limnē (lake, pool) combined with tou pyros (of fire) describes the final destination of the unredeemed. This is not Hades (the intermediate state) but the permanent, eschatological state of judgment. The phrase ho thanatos ho deuteros (the second death) is a Johannine formulation unique to Revelation (2:11; 20:6, 14; 21:8). It contrasts with the 'first death' (physical death) and indicates eternal, spiritual separation from God. That death and Hades themselves are thrown into the lake of fire (v. 14) signifies the abolition of death itself—the last enemy (1 Cor 15:26) is finally destroyed.

panels.ctx: The great white throne judgment (vv. 11–15) is the final, universal judgment of all humanity—the event toward which all previous judgment scenes in Revelation have pointed. The one seated on the throne is so majestic that earth and heaven flee from his presence (v. 11), an image of de-creation that prepares for the new creation of chapter 21. The dead, 'great and small' (v. 12), stand before the throne—no rank, status, or achievement exempts anyone from this judgment. Two sets of books are opened: the books of deeds (recording human actions) and the book of life (recording those who belong to God). Judgment is 'according to what they had done as recorded in the books' (v. 12)—works are the basis of judgment for those not found in the book of life. The sea, death, and Hades give up their dead (v. 13), indicating that no category of the dead is excluded from this reckoning. The climactic act is the casting of death and Hades into the lake of fire (v. 14)—the abolition of death itself, fulfilling 1 Corinthians 15:26 and Isaiah 25:8. Anyone not found in the book of life is thrown into the lake of fire (v. 15)—the second death, the permanent, irreversible separation from God.

cross.note: Daniel's vision of the Ancient of Days taking His seat on a fiery, wheeled throne, with books opened and judgment rendered, provides the primary Old Testament template for the great white throne judgment. The court scene, the opened books, and the rendering of judgment are all present in both passages, establishing continuity between the Danielic and Johannine eschatological visions.

Paul declares that 'the last enemy to be destroyed is death.' Revelation 20:14 fulfills this prophecy: 'Death and Hades were thrown into the lake of fire.' The abolition of death is not merely the cessation of dying but the permanent elimination of death as a power and a reality—it is itself judged and condemned.

Jesus' parable of the sheep and goats presents a final judgment scene where 'all the nations' are gathered before the Son of Man, who separates them based on their treatment of 'the least of these my brothers.' Revelation 20:11–15 expands this into the comprehensive great white throne judgment, where all the dead are judged 'according to what they had done.'

notes.note: The great white throne represents the final, supreme court of divine justice. The one seated is God Himself—so holy, so majestic, that the present heaven and earth flee from His face. The old creation cannot survive in the presence of unmediated divine glory. This is the most solemn scene in all of Scripture: every human being who has ever lived and died apart from saving faith in Christ now stands before their Creator and Judge.

Two categories of books are opened: the books of deeds (recording every thought, word, and action) and the book of life (recording those who belong to God through faith). Works are the evidentiary basis of judgment—not the meritorious cause of salvation. Those in the book of life are saved by grace through faith; those not in the book of life are condemned by the evidence of their works. The sea, death, and Hades give up their dead—no one escapes this final accounting, regardless of how or where they died.

Death and Hades—the intermediate state—are themselves thrown into the lake of fire, signifying the abolition of death forever. The last enemy is destroyed (1 Cor 15:26). Then comes the most fearful verse in the Bible: 'Anyone whose name was not found written in the book of life was thrown into the lake of fire.' This is the second death—eternal, conscious separation from God. There is no third option, no second chance, no annihilation.

The great white throne is the embodiment of God's justice in its final, comprehensive expression. Calvin would stress the universality of this judgment: 'great and small' stand before the throne, confirming that no earthly distinction—wealth, power, fame, obscurity—has any bearing on one's standing before God. The opening of the books reveals that God has kept a perfect record of every human life. This is simultaneously terrifying (for those outside of Christ) and comforting (for those in Christ)—God does not forget, and He does not err.

The destruction of death itself is the ultimate act of divine sovereignty over the created order. Death entered through sin (Gen 3; Rom 5:12); now death itself is condemned. Calvin would see here the final vindication of the resurrection hope: if death is destroyed, then the resurrection is not merely a future event but an accomplished reality in the divine decree. The lake of fire is not God's failure but His completion—the final, permanent separation of evil from the renewed creation that chapter 21 will unveil.

The phrase hou apo tou prosōpou ephygen hē gē kai ho ouranos (from whose face earth and heaven fled) uses the dramatic aorist ephygen (fled) to depict the de-creation of the old order. The verb pheugō (to flee) implies not merely departure but terrified retreat—the created order cannot withstand the unmediated presence of the divine judge. The clause kai topos ouch heurethē autois (and no place was found for them) echoes Daniel 2:35 (Aramaic), where the crushed statue's fragments are blown away and 'no place was found for them.'

The phrase kata ta erga autōn (according to their works) appears five times in Revelation (2:23; 18:6; 20:12, 13; 22:12), establishing works as the consistent standard of divine judgment. The relationship between works-judgment and book-of-life salvation has generated extensive theological discussion. The NET note observes that both criteria operate: the book of life determines destiny; the books of works determine degree.

Beale reads the great white throne as the single, comprehensive judgment of all humanity—both righteous and wicked—at the end of history. The fleeing of earth and heaven (v. 11) is a de-creation motif preparing for the new creation of 21:1: the old order must be dissolved before the new can appear. The two sets of books represent two aspects of the same judgment: the books of deeds reveal the moral character of each life, while the book of life reveals covenant status. For believers, the book of life determines destiny (salvation); the books of deeds determine reward (cf. 1 Cor 3:12–15). For unbelievers, the books of deeds provide the evidentiary basis for condemnation. The casting of death and Hades into the lake of fire (v. 14) is the abolition of the last consequence of the Fall—the final enemy (1 Cor 15:26) is itself subjected to judgment, clearing the way for the deathless new creation.

Osborne identifies the great white throne as the culmination of the judgment theme that has run through Revelation from the letters to the seven churches (chs. 2–3) through the seal, trumpet, and bowl cycles to this final assize. The universality ('great and small'), the comprehensive evidence (books of deeds), and the decisive criterion (book of life) make this the most thorough judgment scene in the Bible. Osborne argues that the relationship between works and grace is not contradictory but complementary: grace saves; works evidence the reality of that salvation. Those whose names are in the book of life are saved by grace; those whose names are absent are condemned by their works, which demonstrate the justice of the verdict. The destruction of death (v. 14) is the eschatological fulfillment of Isaiah 25:8 ('He will swallow up death forever') and Paul's triumph cry (1 Cor 15:54–55: 'Where, O death, is your victory?').

themes.note: Revelation 20 is dominated by sovereignty, judgment, and justice as God brings evil to its final resolution. The millennium debate should not obscure the chapter's central affirmation: evil is temporary, divine justice is comprehensive, and death itself will be destroyed. The great white throne ensures that no moral account goes unsettled.

rows.label: vv.1-3
rows.text: Satan bound for a thousand years

rows.label: vv.4-6
rows.text: First resurrection; saints reign with Christ

rows.label: vv.7-8
rows.text: Satan released; Gog and Magog gathered

rows.label: v.9
rows.text: Fire from heaven devours the rebels

rows.label: v.10
rows.text: Devil cast into the lake of fire

rows.label: v.13
rows.text: Sea, death, Hades give up their dead

rows.label: vv.14-15
rows.text: Death destroyed; second death for those not in book of life

lit.note: Revelation 20 divides into three panels: the millennium (vv. 1–6), Satan's final rebellion (vv. 7–10), and the great white throne judgment (vv. 11–15). Each panel resolves a different dimension of evil: the millennium addresses Satan's deception (binding), the rebellion addresses human corruption (fire from heaven), and the throne addresses cosmic accountability (universal judgment). The progression moves from temporary restraint to permanent resolution.

hebtext.note: The phrase chilia etē (a thousand years) appears six times in this passage (vv. 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7), making it the most concentrated use of a specific time period in Revelation. The Old Testament background includes Psalm 90:4 ('a thousand years in your sight are like a day') and the rabbinic tradition of a messianic sabbath following six millennia of history.

The phrase hē anastasis hē prōtē (the first resurrection) is unique to this passage and generates intense interpretive debate. The names Gōg kai Magōg derive from Ezekiel 38–39, where Gog is a ruler from the land of Magog who leads a vast coalition against Israel in the latter days, only to be utterly destroyed by divine intervention. The adjective megas (great) indicates supreme authority; leukos (white) indicates purity and righteousness. Together, they describe a throne of absolute, unimpeachable justice. The singular thronos contrasts with the plural thronoi of 20:4—there, delegated authority; here, ultimate authority.
